pages.raw_text:
KANSAS CITY, Mo-, Jan. g
When the people of Atlanta, little
town in southern Kansas, with but
one railway, stopped through
freight train and gave this order
to the conductor, Take out car
of coal and allow us enough to keep
warm" they struck at the heart of
the car shortage problem. The car
was set out: coal was distributed
to those needing it, and remittance
was made to the company.


If a railroad company may con-
fiscate coal, why not town?


The car problem is vital to the
west. The soul of it is not always
reached in hotel parlors where high
salaried officials make 'investiga-
tons" To understand it go out in
the "short grass country. A scat
tered prairie town, one small ele-
vator, one or two coal yards with
storage for two or three cars of
coal at a time. Few granaries for
the wheat, few families with capital
enough to lay in the winters coal
in September. Cars must come
when they are wanted-or there is
distress.


In the north billiards threaten,
in the south, cattle and sheep await
shipment to market. On branch
lines in the wheat belt when cars
cannot be had, wheat is heaped on
the ground, 10000 bushels in a
single pile.


II tell you. we fellows in Texas
have our blood up" declared H. c..
Jet, of Fort Worth, a leading cat
tleman. eve are ready to tear up
tracks; we can't standt it." He has
lost $4,000 through lack of cars in
which to ship cattle.


Col Mudro M'Kenzie, president
of the American Live Stock associa-
ton, who has taken the matter up
officially, puts it this way: 1--WE
Texans have to wait sometimes two
months for cars. Cattle and sheep
are driven from Texas to the north
because there are no cars. | lost
| 81 900 on one shipment because of
a wait of six weeks-yet hundreds
of stock cars are backed on coal
